metadata.notes: Observed inputs update from Google Trends, the World Bank API, and the Hansen-style remote-work workbook or its exported snapshot. Right-side fertility, mental-health, and productivity panels are model-implied pressure scores rather than causal estimates.
metadata.status_note: Google Trends nowcasts were refreshed on June 22, 2026; World Bank fertility and population-growth baselines are 2024 and 2024; the remote-work anchor uses 10.1% remote postings in March 2026.

live_panel.note: Observed cards mix two kinds of measures: direct anchors from the remote-work workbook and official fertility/population series, plus Google Trends-based nowcasts for digital and in-person attention. Estimated adult shares are calibration-based, not direct census counts.

current_stats.title: Adults with high non-work screen time daily
current_stats.note: Estimated share built from social, GenAI, and in-person attention mix.

current_stats.title: Adults using GenAI at home weekly
current_stats.note: Nowcast from Google Trends for ChatGPT.

current_stats.title: Adults using dating apps monthly
current_stats.note: Nowcast from Google Trends for Tinder.

current_stats.title: Adults using social media daily
current_stats.note: Nowcast from Google Trends for Instagram.

current_stats.title: Adults with weekly in-person social routines
current_stats.note: Nowcast from Google Trends for restaurants near me.

relationship_split.label: Single adults
relationship_split.note: Modeled split: singles are weighted toward app-based matching and solitary digital time, so the mental-health and fertility margins are more exposed when in-person routines thin out.

relationship_split.label: Married or cohabiting adults
relationship_split.note: Modeled split: partnered adults have less exposure to the matching margin, but fertility timing can still fall if screen-heavy home life crowds out energy, local routines, or community support.

balance.home_digital_note: Weighted average of GenAI, dating, and social-media nowcasts.
balance.in_person_note: Weighted average of on-site work and in-person social nowcasts.
balance.summary: The core margin is simple: when home-based digital intensity rises above in-person anchors, the model tilts toward later partnering, more isolation pressure, and a wider productivity gap between AI-complementary and less-complementary work.

regions.name: Northeast
regions.summary: Northeast is below the U.S. GenAI nowcast and below the U.S. in-person activity proxy. That leaves a weaker home-shift gap than the national balance.

fertility.summary: If this home-shift mix persisted, the dashboard model moves the region toward a 1.68 fertility midpoint and about 1.19% population growth.
fertility.takeaway: The sign can still reverse: remote flexibility can help partnered households, but heavy digital-at-home substitution usually lowers the meeting, matching, and coordination margin.

mental_health.takeaway: This panel follows the Emma Harrington-style margin: days spent alone, sadness or depression, and mental-health care use rise when home-based digital time outpaces face-to-face routines.

productivity.takeaway: GenAI looks more complementary in already-remote-capable work, while on-site service and manual jobs benefit less from the home shift and can lose coordination or routine.

scenarios.title: Digital life outruns local routines
scenarios.trigger: Home digital share exceeds in-person anchors by 0.0%
scenarios.summary: This is the downside path: more life shifts onto screens without enough local work or social routines to offset it.
scenarios.fertility: Model midpoint slips toward about 1.63 births per woman.
scenarios.mental_health: Days alone and sadness or depression tilt upward fastest for adults living alone.
scenarios.productivity: Skilled workers still gain from GenAI, but the skilled-unskilled productivity gap widens.

scenarios.title: Flexibility supports coordination
scenarios.trigger: Remote posting anchor stays near 10.1% while in-person social routines hold up
scenarios.summary: This is the upside path: remote flexibility saves time, but workers still keep face-to-face anchors and community contact.
scenarios.fertility: Model midpoint can edge back toward about 1.63 births per woman.
scenarios.mental_health: The isolation margin softens because digital work does not fully replace coworkers, friends, and outside routines.
scenarios.productivity: GenAI complementarity remains positive without as much mental-health drag on the rest of the labor market.

method_snapshot.title: Google Trends nowcast
method_snapshot.text: Representative terms are ChatGPT, Tinder, Instagram, restaurants near me, and remote jobs. Each proxy is normalized by its own U.S. 12-month mean.

method_snapshot.title: Remote work anchor
method_snapshot.text: Pulled from the Hansen-style workbook in March 2026; the dashboard uses 1 minus this share as the national on-site work anchor.

method_snapshot.title: Fertility baseline
method_snapshot.text: Official World Bank U.S. baselines for 2024 fertility and 2024 population growth.

method_snapshot.title: Mental-health outcomes
method_snapshot.text: The projection layer follows the remote-work mental-health margin emphasized in the Emma Harrington coauthored Science paper.

method_snapshot.title: Productivity split
method_snapshot.text: Skilled and unskilled productivity indices are anchored to occupation-level remote-capability differences in the workbook, then tilted by regional GenAI and in-person proxies.
